bids.reasoning: This is the opening bid at t=0, so I should start with my maximum utility offer. My top values across all issues give me the highest possible utility: issueA=valueF (1.0), issueB=valueF (1.0), issueC=valueF (1.0), issueD=valueF (1.0). This is the natural opening position — anchor high and concede gradually as time progresses and I learn the opponent's preferences.

The opponent opened with their best offer (all valueA), which gives me very low utility (0.1*0.20 + 0.2*0.20 + 0.3*0.20 + 0.3*0.20 = 0.20). We're very early (t=0.02), so I should stay close to my top utility bid. My opening was all valueF (utility = 1.0). I'll make a very minor concession on issueA (my least important issue at 0.1 weight) by dropping to valueE, keeping everything else at valueF to signal I'm willing to negotiate but not capitulate early.

The opponent is stubbornly offering all valueA (minimum utility for me) and hasn't moved at all after two rounds. It's very early (t=0.04), so I have no pressure to concede significantly. I'll stay near my top utility while making a small concession on issueA (low weight for me) to signal willingness to negotiate. I'll hold firm on issueC and issueD (highest weights) and make a minor concession on issueB.

The opponent is stubbornly holding near their best values (mostly valueA) but just shifted issueB to valueD, which suggests they care about issueB. My highest-weight issues are issueD and issueC (0.3 each), so I should hold firm there. The opponent seems to prefer low values (valueA) across the board. I'll concede slightly on issueA (low weight for me) and issueB, while keeping issueC and issueD at high utility values. I'll try issueB: valueD to match their signal there, and keep issueC: valueF and issueD: valueF since those are my most important issues.

The opponent has been mostly anchored at valueA for most issues, but their latest offer shows some movement: issueB moved to valueD (which I already matched), issueC moved to valueD, and issueD moved to valueC. This suggests they care about issueA and issueB less (or prefer low values there), while showing some flexibility on issueC and issueD. Since issueC and issueD are my highest-weight issues (0.3 each), I should hold firm on those. I'll keep issueC and issueD at valueF (my top values), and concede slightly on issueA (low weight for me) while keeping issueB at a good value. My current utility target remains high (~0.9+) since we're only at t=0.08.

The opponent's latest offer gives me: issueA: valueB (0.28×0.1=0.028), issueB: valueA (0.20×0.2=0.04), issueC: valueD (0.79×0.3=0.237), issueD: valueF (1.00×0.3=0.30) = ~0.605 utility. That's actually decent for me! The opponent seems to be conceding on issueD (now valueF, my top value) and issueC (valueD). I should respond with a bid that holds firm on my high-weight issues (issueC and issueD at top values) while conceding a bit more on low-weight issues. I'll keep issueD: valueF and issueC: valueF (my best), concede issueB to valueC or valueD, and issueA to valueB. This is still early (t=0.10), so I don't need to rush concessions.

The opponent's latest offer (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueC, issueC: valueC, issueD: valueF) gives me utility: 0.1*0.20 + 0.2*0.50 + 0.3*0.93 + 0.3*1.00 = 0.02 + 0.10 + 0.279 + 0.30 = 0.699. That's actually decent. The opponent seems to be conceding on issueD (now valueF, which is my top) and issueC (now valueC, which is 0.93 for me). I should hold firm on issueC and issueD (my highest weights) while making small concessions on issueA and issueB. I'll offer issueA: valueA (conceding fully on low-weight issue), issueB: valueC, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF to get utility: 0.1*0.20 + 0.2*0.50 + 0.3*1.00 + 0.3*1.00 = 0.02 + 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.30 = 0.72, while being more attractive to the opponent who seems to want low values on issueA and issueB.

The opponent has been mostly holding firm on valueA for most issues but has now conceded issueD to valueF (which is my top value) and issueB to valueC. Their latest offer gives me: issueA(valueD)=0.1*0.62, issueB(valueC)=0.2*0.50, issueC(valueA)=0.3*0.20, issueD(valueF)=0.3*1.00 = 0.062+0.10+0.06+0.30 = 0.522. I should hold firm on issueC and issueD (my highest weight issues) while making small concessions on issueA and issueB. The opponent seems to care a lot about issueC (always pushing valueA there), so I'll stay at valueF for issueC and issueD, and try valueC for issueB and valueA for issueA to show movement.

The opponent's latest offer (issueA: valueA, issueB: valueF, issueC: valueD, issueD: valueF) gives me utility: 0.1*0.20 + 0.2*1.00 + 0.3*0.79 + 0.3*1.00 = 0.02 + 0.20 + 0.237 + 0.30 = 0.757. That's actually quite good for me! The opponent seems to have settled on issueD: valueF (which is my top value) and is now offering issueB: valueF. I should try to get issueC higher while keeping issueD: valueF and issueB: valueF. I'll offer issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF, issueB: valueF, and concede a bit on issueA (low weight). This is still early game so I can hold firm on my high-weight issues.

The opponent's last offer (t=0.18) is {issueA: valueA, issueB: valueC, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF}. Let me calculate my utility for this: issueA(0.1*0.20) + issueB(0.2*0.50) + issueC(0.3*1.00) + issueD(0.3*1.00) = 0.02 + 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.30 = 0.72. That's actually quite good for me! The opponent seems to have converged on issueC: valueF and issueD: valueF (both my top values), and is flexible on issueB. We're still early (t=0.18), so I shouldn't rush to accept, but I should make a competitive counter that keeps issueC and issueD at valueF while trying to improve issueB slightly. I'll offer issueB: valueE to try to get a bit more utility while staying close to what the opponent offered.

The opponent's last offer (t=0.20) is: issueA: valueA, issueB: valueC, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF. Let me calculate my utility for this: issueA(0.1*0.20) + issueB(0.2*0.50) + issueC(0.3*1.00) + issueD(0.3*1.00) = 0.02 + 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.30 = 0.72. That's actually quite good for me! The opponent seems to have converged on issueC: valueF and issueD: valueF (which are my top values for the two most important issues). They want issueB: valueC and issueA: valueA. I should try to get issueB higher while keeping issueC and issueD at valueF. Since we're only at t=0.20, I can still push for issueB: valueE or valueF while conceding issueA fully. My current bid at t=0.18 was issueB: valueE - let me try to find a middle ground. The opponent keeps returning to issueB: valueC, so I'll try issueB: valueD as a compromise while holding issueC and issueD at valueF.

The opponent has been consistently offering issueA: valueA, issueB: valueC, issueC: valueF, issueD: valueF in their last several bids. This gives me utility: 0.1*0.20 + 0.2*0.50 + 0.3*1.00 + 0.3*1.00 = 0.02 + 0.10 + 0.30 + 0.30 = 0.72. That's actually quite good for me! The opponent seems to have settled on this pattern. I should try to get a bit more on issueB (my weight is 0.2) while keeping issueC and issueD at valueF. I'll offer issueB: valueC (matching what they want) to signal convergence, but I can try valueD or valueE on issueB. Actually, the opponent keeps asking for issueB: valueC, and my utility for valueC is 0.50 vs valueD is 0.51 - nearly the same. I should just accept their pattern essentially, but try to push issueB slightly higher. Let me offer issueB: valueD to keep my utility slightly higher while being close to what they want.
